trajectory_summary: The call opens cleanly and moves through verify at a functional pace, but it never builds energy — it processes. The one proactive moment (tree/shading check at 03:14) is real but isolated. The call collapses in the bill-collection phase not because verbal readback failed, but because the rep abandoned a working verbal path to chase a photo, which ended in a FaceTime accident. No appointment was booked.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is 'unknown' — based on the transcript, the call ended without a booked appointment and without a confirmed callback time. The outcome is an unbooked lead with an unresolved bill-collection attempt. If v1 tagged this as any form of booking or tentative, that would be a miscall.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Stay on the working path — verbal readback was succeeding
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Current reading, 7863. Previous reading, usage, yes, 1482.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Bucky had the bill data coming in verbally at 05:34. The customer was cooperative, on the portal, reading numbers aloud. The pivot to a photo request at 05:48 abandoned a working channel, created friction the customer flagged immediately ('It's on my monitor'), and ended in a FaceTime accident that collapsed the call. The coaching is not 'pivot to verbal' — it is 'you were already on verbal; recognize when a path is working and stay on it.' This is the single most recoverable failure on the call: one different decision at 05:48 likely produces a booked appointment.

key_moments.stewart_read: Customer discloses a co-owner — her daughter — at 00:32. Rep says 'Okay' and moves immediately to utility company. No follow-up on the daughter: is she a co-decision-maker? Is she reachable for the appointment? The schema is explicit that a co-owner on title must be included in the appointment. Strategic read: rep may have planned to address this at button-up. Reactive read: rep missed the trigger entirely and never returned to it. Since no appointment was booked and no button-up occurred, the strategic read has no supporting evidence.

key_moments.quote: that tree on, like, the south side of your house, are you willing to trim it up a bit if we can offer something good?
key_moments.stewart_read: Rep is working from satellite imagery and proactively checking south-side shading — the one genuinely strategic move on the call. The customer's response is a strong enthusiasm signal: 'There is no tree at this time. All the trees have been trimmed or removed.' Clean south-facing exposure with no shading is a high-value qualification signal. Rep says 'Okay. Sounds good' and moves on. The signal warranted a brief anchor: this home's roof profile is favorable, which is exactly what the closer needs to build a compelling design.

Rep captures the bill number and moves on. The schema's bill_anchoring pattern is capture + acknowledge + flip. Rep does capture ('Yeah') but neither acknowledges the pain nor flips it. '$200 on a small house' is actually a useful data point — it tells the closer the usage profile is modest, which affects system sizing. But the emotional opportunity is also present: even a $200 bill is $2,400/year going to the utility. Rep left the flip on the table.

key_moments.quote: Current reading, 7863. Previous reading, usage, yes, 1482.
key_moments.stewart_read: Verbal readback was working. The customer navigated to the Dominion portal, found the usage screen, and was reading numbers aloud. This is the bill_collection.alternative_paths.elderly_low_tech_protocol in action — the customer self-selected into it. The coaching point is not 'pivot to verbal' (the schema's standard note for this protocol); it is 'you were already on verbal — stay there.' Rep instead pivoted to a photo request at 05:48, which the customer flagged as awkward ('It's on my monitor') and which ended in a FaceTime accident.
